data.title: Olaplex: Buying The Takeover News
data.description:
With the news that Olaplex (OLPX) has received a takeover offer and the subsequent 20% jump in its stock price on January 7th, how does an investor or potential investor view the prospects of the stock? Has the move already been made, or is there still room to run? Acquisitions can be binary results for investors, as stock prices may rocket higher when a deal is announced but collapse just as quickly if an offer doesn’t come to fruition. Consequently, a savvy investor has to be sure that the company in question is sound and worthy of investment absent the acquisition. In my opinion, I believe Olaplex has stabilized and can be a good investment at the current price with or without a deal. At these levels, there’s still room for significant upside potential, but the downside is underpinned by the company’s positive cash flows and momentum established by the new management team.

New Management Team in Place

I have been impressed with the new management team that was put in place over the past two years. It’s not an easy task to turn around a company that was very clearly struggling, as quarterly revenues were cut in half from their highs of mid-2022, but that was the mission for new CEO Amanda Baldwin and team after she took over in early 2024. This was the cliché falling knife, and no one had any idea how far the fall was going to be. Looking at the last 15 quarters of sales, it does appear that some stabilization has finally occurred:

More than that, though, is the way it has occurred. There’s no magic formula or lever to pull, so Baldwin began with a top-down approach by launching a strategic plan in early 2025, “Bonds and Beyond,” after spending the first year understanding the company and her customers. Many will see this strategic plan as a very “corporate” move and one that is not all that substantive, and I do agree that companies have very mixed track records with programs such as these. One only needs to look in the same industry at Estee Lauder’s disastrous “Profit Recovery and Growth Plan” as an example of what not to do. With that piece of skepticism in mind, I do think it is important to get the entire company moving in one direction. But it is not just about vision; it is also about tactics and execution. And that is where I see a glimmer of hope. An example of this was the launch of two hair mask products in late August that have outperformed expectations, according to CEO Amanda Baldwin in the Q3 2025 conference call. Olaplex’s foundation is based on scientific innovation, not just producing more of the same product for a consumer to replace every three to six months. This product launch’s 360 degree marketing effort was well coordinated with professionals, beauty influencers, pop-up stores, and a dizzying PR campaign that appears to have been effective at creating a buzz for the new products.

Creating demand is the first tenet of the new strategic vision, and so far it appears that they have managed to do so, exceeding Q3 revenues and earnings expectations. Absent any acquisition news, I believe that Q4 earnings and, more importantly, 2026 guidance are major milestones and a window into whether Olaplex will continue to stumble, or finally break through and achieve the momentum that was lost a few years back with the negative publicity associated with the hair loss lawsuit that was eventually dismissed.

A Potential Suitor

News broke Wednesday afternoon that Henkel (HENKY) had submitted a takeover offer to acquire Olaplex, with a deal potentially coming in the next few weeks.

Henkel is a EUR 21 billion German company that has been around for 150 years and has acquired many consumer brands in many different industries. One of those industries is personal care, and that specifically includes 15 hair care brands, with the largest being Schwarzkopf. Olaplex seems to be a natural fit with their current brands, and financially, a deal would not be that taxing based on the billions of cash currently on Henkel's balance sheet. Could the news leaking impact the amount Henkel is willing to pay? That’s certainly a possibility given the recent stock price spike, but I can’t imagine the Olaplex Board would be entertaining an acquisition price that could be thwarted by a 20% move in the stock. It might result in a lower premium on the actual announcement date, but it is likely to be far higher than 20% of the trading price of Olaplex before this news broke.

Valuation and Potential Premium

What type of premium would Olaplex require in order to actually sell the company? Normally, one would assume that at a minimum 30-40% is an acceptable deal premium, but is that really the case for a company that dropped in price from $22 to the current price of approximately $1.65, having dropped even lower only a few months ago? Would the board accept an acquisition price of, say, $2.30 (which is just shy of 40% above $1.65), or would this not be in the long-term interests of shareholders? How would the 20% jump on Wednesday impact all of these discussions?

But before we just assume that a deal is going to get done, let’s look at Olaplex’s valuation as a standalone entity. As an investor, you HAVE to be comfortable with the company as it currently stands because an acquisition is uncertain at best, even with the recent news reported. As I previously wrote, Olaplex’s earnings are hampered by substantial intangible amortization, which makes looking at a price to earnings ratio very difficult. I generally prefer enterprise value to free cash flow anyway, but especially in cases such as this, given the intangible amortization. Currently, EV/FCF is approximately 18.5x based on a stock price of $1.65. That’s a little on the high side, but not terrible in this market for a company of this size. When you add the possibility of an acquisition, I wouldn’t hesitate to pay this amount right now.

Risks

So what happens if a deal is not consummated? Is an investor left with a company that is down 93% from its IPO and struggling to make money? Well, as mentioned above, I do believe that management is turning the business around, and Q4 earnings and the 2026 outlook are major touchpoints in determining how successful that turnaround is going. If a deal doesn’t happen, the stock is most likely going to give back Wednesday’s gains, but I don’t think all is lost for Olaplex investors. The company continues to have positive earnings (though just barely) and positive free cash flow that it can use to reinvigorate its business through continued marketing investments.

I think the biggest risk of all is if the company doesn’t get acquired and Q4 and/or the 2026 outlook is weak. At that point one must question whether the strategic plan is taking hold at all. While the signs and momentum appear to be there, the Company must execute in the short term to reinforce that they are on the right track.

Conclusion

A rumored acquisition is not necessarily a reason to buy a stock, but I do think that there are other positives to Olaplex’s business that can mitigate the downside should the deal not come to fruition. With the new management team in place for well over a year now and coming off a stronger than expected third quarter, Olaplex is in a good position to turn around its business and build momentum into 2026. A potential acquisition offers a strong upside for investors and speculators alike.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Retire With A Potential $5,000 Monthly Income And High Growth
data.description:
Introduction:

It is that time of the year when a lot of people make new resolutions only to forget them in a few weeks or sometimes a couple of months. To plan for retirement, there is no better time than today, especially if you have crossed the 50-year mark. However, someone famous once said, "Plans are useless; planning is essential". So, unless a plan is backed by proper planning, it may prove to be futile. You may have many plans for retirement, such as pursuing your hobbies, touring the world, or downsizing and settling down in a retirement-friendly place. But all said and done, they may remain distant goals unless you prepare a blueprint for how you are going to achieve them. In fact, the planning process basically involves defining your goals and sub-goals and preparing a blueprint of your detailed action plan. Also needed is a process to measure your progress and success rate on a periodic basis. If the progress is not satisfactory, the action plan may need to be modified/adjusted.

Retirement Planning:

First, you need to define clear-cut goals, action plans, and a process for how to implement them. Preferably, consider defining the best-case and the worst-case scenarios. A realistic scenario will fall somewhere between the two. Also, divide your goals into smaller periodic goals to be able to measure the success rate. For example, if you are planning to save $500,000 (excluding growth) over the next 20 years, divide this over every 5-year period as well as on an annual basis. When it comes to growth, it may be difficult to define annual goals, as outcomes would depend greatly on the market environment, but 5-year goals may still be useful.

You may like to start with the following:

  • The amount of capital you need to accumulate prior to retirement to meet your retirement objectives.
  • The time horizon for achieving retirement goals.
  • The fallback or alternative options in case of a shortfall.

The amount of retirement capital that is needed would vary greatly from person to person. It all depends on your own expenses and the acceptable living standard that you are accustomed to. Obviously, the time factor (the number of years between now and the age at which you would like to retire) is important. The more time you have, the easier the path will be. That's why most financial planners would emphasize starting early. Also, flexibility is important, as there are many factors that are outside your control, such as the market and economic environment, as well as unforeseeable events in the future. For example, if your goal was to retire at age 65, you should be prepared to retire early as well as be ready to work a bit longer.

Once the primary and critical factors and goals have been defined, it may be time to write down secondary goals and financial parameters. For example, what is the minimum (or maximum) income that you should be aiming to withdraw from your portfolio? Another example would be to define your tolerance for drawdowns and corrections. This one is tough to define, as most folks overestimate their capacity to tolerate drawdowns without panicking. Then there are smaller issues, such as whether to invest in funds or individual stocks.

Below, we will lay out a planning process and an investment portfolio for a hypothetical investor; however, we will try to make it somewhat generalized and flexible so that it would suit a large number of folks with their own customization.

The portfolio presented should suit investors at any age, but this would be especially useful for folks who are 10 to 15 (preferably 15) years away from retirement.

So, let's define our financial goals:

  • 5% to 6% income level
  • Overall, 10% or better growth of the capital
  • Safety of the capital- drawdowns to be 70-80% of the S&P500
  • Minimal maintenance effort.

Income may not be important initially, but gradually, as you approach retirement, the importance of income takes center stage. So, we will form a portfolio that is appropriate for both stages, pre-retirement and post-retirement. Moreover, over the long term (10-15 years), we should expect our portfolio to provide the necessary growth that matches the long-term growth of the stock market. In addition, we want our portfolio to be less volatile and have lower drawdowns than the broader market. This is a very desirable feature, especially for conservative investors. Lastly, but not least, we want the portfolio to require minimal effort to maintain.

Portfolio Structure:

We will have three components (or buckets) in this portfolio:

  • A Buy-and-Hold Funds-based DGI bucket: – 50% allocation
  • A Hedging/Rotation Bucket—30% Allocation.
  • A Buy-and-Hold Asset-Diversified Bucket—20% Allocation

Though we have specified the allocations to each bucket, this is highly flexible, and each investor should adjust the allocations based on their income needs, risk tolerance, and personal preferences. Each bucket has its specific function and purpose (discussed below) in the overall portfolio.

The second bucket is a "Hedging" or "Rotational" bucket. Basically, the goal is to provide an in-built safety (partial) mechanism against deep drawdowns and keep the overall volatility lower than the broader market. If the broader market drops by 30%, we expect the rotational bucket to fall only by half or two-thirds; however, there are no guarantees of future circumstances. The downside of the rotational bucket is that it will require some effort (and trading) on a monthly basis. Due to monthly trading, it works best inside a tax-deferred account. We expect this bucket to provide between 4%-6% income and market-matching or exceeding growth.

The third bucket (with 20% allocation) is of the buy-and-hold type. It will be invested in 5 securities that represent different types of asset classes. The idea is that they are likely to have some variance in their behavior under different economic environments.

The overall structure of the portfolio provides a lot of flexibility in terms of how many buckets we may want to have or what allocations we want to assign to each bucket. If someone wants to have a hands-off approach, they can simply have the two buy-and-hold buckets and avoid the rotational bucket. These buckets are the building blocks of the framework that you can use to form your own portfolio to suit your individual needs.

(I) A Funds-Based DGI and Income Bucket: 50% Allocation

This bucket is the core bucket of this portfolio, and that's why we are suggesting allocating half of the total capital. This bucket will also produce a reasonably high income, with an average of 6% yield.

We recently published an article about a buy-and-hold fund-based portfolio with 10 funds. Here is the link to the article. We will use the same (or similar) portfolio for this bucket. Here is the portfolio with the current income yield. You could read more details on individual funds in the above article.

The securities are (SCHD), (ADX), (GPIQ), (BST), (RNP), (GLDM), (VAW), (PEO), (AMLP), and (UTG).

Table-1:

(II) The Hedging (ROTATIONAL) Bucket: (30% Allocation)

With 50% of the portfolio allocated to a DGI-oriented fund-based bucket, we will now introduce a bucket that will provide hedging and reduce volatility and drawdowns and provide some stability during times of panic and deep corrections. We will allocate 30% of the total funds to this bucket.

For this monthly rotational bucket, we will choose FIVE core securities, one each from a different asset class with a low correlation with each other. However, we will select only two securities every month for investment for the following month. There will also be a hedging security that would replace the core securities if some conditions are met (explained below).

Table-2: The list of securities:

As we can see, three out of the five securities are closed-end funds, and they generally provide high income. However, there is a fifth security (10-year mid-term Treasury) that acts as a fallback or hedging security.

Rotation Method: Every month, we will calculate the total performance returns (including any distributions) over the previous three months and seven months for each of the five securities. We will assign 50% weightage to each of the two periods (3-month and 7-month performance) to calculate the average performance. We will select the two top securities that have performed the highest in terms of total returns (including any dividends/distributions). If the performance of any of the two selected securities is lower than the risk-free return provided by cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then it is replaced with the IEF-Treasury fund for the next month; otherwise, it is retained. This process is repeated at the end of each month.

Some folks may find this rotation method somewhat complex, and that is certainly a negative. However, it can provide high growth, moderate monthly income, usually 3-4%, and much lower drawdowns than the S&P 500.

(III) Buy-and-Hold Bucket, with the same (or similar) assets as the Rotation bucket: (20% Allocation)

With 50% and 30% allocated to DGI and Rotation strategies, we will now allocate 20% to another buy-and-hold bucket. This bucket will provide the simplicity of buy-and-hold but provide high growth, moderate income, and diversity of many different asset classes. The idea behind this bucket is that these securities will have significant variance among themselves as they come from many different asset classes.

Please note that this portfolio may be as volatile (or sometimes more) as the S&P 500. But based on past performance, it is likely to outperform the S&P 500. The securities are the same as the rotation bucket, but they are held on a buy-and-hold basis and in equal proportion.

The list of securities:

  • ProShares Ultra QQQ ETF (QLD) [or Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ)]
  • Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX)
  • Kayne Anderson Energy Infra Fund (KYN)
  • Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI)
  • SPDR Gold Trust (GLD).

The Combined Portfolio:

Now, let's see how the overall portfolio would behave. Though the future will always be somewhat different than the past, backtesting can provide us with some general idea. We already have the test results from buckets 2 and 3. To test bucket 1 for 15 years or more, we will need to make some replacements. For example, we will allocate the share of SCHD to ADX, AMLP to Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and replace GPIQ and BST with some other similar fund, such as Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (EOS).

Here is the comparative chart for the growth of $100,000 put in the combined model portfolio versus the S&P 500. As per the backtesting results, the combined portfolio would have provided an annualized growth rate of 14% since 2008, compared to 10.9% for the S&P 500.

Chart-1:

Risks:

There are always risks with any stock investments. The portfolio described above has a high concentration in equity investments. Though there are some bonds and fixed-income investments, the equity investments take the majority share. The portfolio employs a hedging strategy in the form of a rotational bucket; however, the allocation is at best 1/3 of the overall portfolio, and even so, any hedging mechanism has limitations. Also, it may be worth pointing out that there may be some level of duplication of some stocks between the first two buckets; however, we think it should be minor. Further, no stock portfolio can be totally immune to deep corrections or impacts from a recession. We believe the income from the portfolio should help in such situations, but income may also fall to some extent in a prolonged recession.

Concluding Thoughts:

In our view, the model portfolio presented in this article is highly diversified, provides reasonable income for most folks, and is simple enough to implement.

The combined model portfolio will provide roughly 5.5% to 6% income, or roughly $60,000 annually ($5,000 monthly) on a $1 million portfolio. We think one should not aim to withdraw more than 6%, as that would impact the long-term growth of the portfolio. Also, an income-based portfolio makes the ride much smoother, as there would be no need to sell shares during a market correction or at the bottom.

That being said, many readers may find the rotational bucket a bit complex. If that is the case, you could forgo the rotation bucket and look for some other hedging alternatives. One such example would be the JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered ETF (HELO). But the fund has 0.50% expenses, provides a very low-income yield, and overall growth may be inferior to the rotation method.

data.title: Alphabet's 'Ironwood' Singularity And The Inevitability Of The Zero-Marginal-Cost Compute Flywheel
data.description:
Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) stock is a strong buy for me. Alphabet has a highly positive risk-reward profile among the Magnificent 7. I am saying this not just because of Google’s search monopoly, but because Alphabet can be the largest vertically integrated AI company. It is different from Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) that remain beholden to the ‘Nvidia (NVDA) Tax’ on compute. Alphabet has closed the loop on the AI value chain. Alphabet now owns the energy (nuclear/geothermal deals), the silicon (7th Gen TPUs/Ironwood), the infrastructure (Google Cloud/Data Centers), the model (Gemini), and the distribution (Android/Chrome/YouTube).

So, in my opinion, Wall Street is currently mispricing Alphabet as a legacy ad utility facing disruption and not pricing in its transition into a hardware-software hybrid AI-comp capable of compressing inference costs faster than others. The primary risk is not technological obsolescence here, and it is also not the regulatory fracture, as it cannot sever the distribution arm/line for Gemini. However, the real risk is the Agentic disintermediation of ads. Hence, GOOG’s current pricing offers a free option on Waymo’s autonomous market lead and the margin expansion from custom silicon. The update from BofA on the daily active users data on leading models moves me to assess Alphabet stock upside and risks.

GOOG Stock Forward Technicals Indicate A ~90% Price Upside In FY2026

GOOG’s weekly price chart holds the Fibonacci Golden Spiral breakout on a multi-year basis. The pattern signals that GOOG is exiting a multi-week consolidation phase and entering an impulse wave (Elliott Wave). The current price is now above a critical resistance (now support) point that is the upper bound of the Trend Pivot’s Range of Influence. A stable uptrend along with a diagonal trendline (grey) will clearly invalidate bearish theses and initiate a high-pace move toward $427 (35% price upside) near the 2.118 Fibonacci extension. However, there can be an extended consolidation over/around $295, and the momentum indicators mark this possibility. The MACD (7.59) is crossing down into bearish as the histogram is weakening on the longer-bullish-mode and may enter a shorter-bearish mode.

A consolidation can accelerate buying pressure, as a stabilizing RSI (77.70) over 70 is pointing to solid bullish strength instead of exhaustion. It is a mark of super-cycle moves where the stock remains overbought for extended durations (during price discovery). Side-by-side, the stochastic oscillator at 91.73 has stabilized for more than 2 quarters and confirms this embedded high bullish momentum. Now, why am I saying the longer-bullish-mode and shorter-bearish-mode (super-cycle), because RSI and Stochastic oscillator have dropped quickly towards oversold states (30 and 20) (see Q3-2024 and Q1-2025) and then resumed trend reversion constructively/slowly towards overbought levels over the mid-term.

The main point here is that there is a terminal velocity target of $599 (90% price upside) based on the golden ratio (near 3.618 extension) for FY2026. The diagonal trend initiated in early Q2 suggests that GOOG stock price is technically running up under the realization of AI monetization over 6-week (dark yellow) and 13-week (red) EMAs. The main support floor has shifted higher to the $295 level (1.0 extension), so any pullbacks toward this level, the EMAs ($296-$313 range), and trend-pivot (at $234) can provide a highly favorable risk/reward accumulation zone to average down the long position after the main buy at the current price.

The Zero-Marginal-Cost Compute Flywheel

The unit-economics decoupling through TPU maturation (the Ironwood 7th Gen architecture) is the long-term bullish catalyst for GOOG/GOOGL stock price. Although Wall Street is tilted on earnings beat on revenue and EPS, in my opinion, the lever for Alphabet’s forward valuation expansion is the collapse of its Internal Cost of Compute [ICC] relative to Microsoft (OPENAI) and Meta as they engaged in a CapEx race. A heavy percentage of Microsoft’s, OpenAI’s, and Meta’s every dollar of CapEx spent flows directly to Nvidia/AMD (AMD), capping their long-term gross margins on AI services and at consolidated levels. Google’s 7th Gen TPU (Ironwood) delivers a 10x peak-performance improvement over TPU v5p and 4x better performance per chip. This is a deflationary tool as AI models shift from training (capital-intensive) to inference (OpEx), the cost to serve a query becomes the determinant of profitability.

For more clarity, in my opinion, Google’s ability to run Gemini on internal silicon can drive the marginal cost of an AI search query toward zero far faster than Bing and ChatGPT, which are shackled to commercial GPU pricing and margins. This silicon advantage creates a Compute Flywheel. First, it leads to lower inference costs, as Google can afford to infuse heavy AI models (like Gemini 3 Deep Think) into free products (AI Studio, Search, YouTube, Gmail) without destroying margins. Direct competitors may not match this without incurring losses/charging relatively higher subscription fees, in my opinion. The second thing is Data Gravity. Lower costs allow Google to process more tokens (already processing 1.3 quadrillion monthly tokens, +20x YoY) than the competitors. This massive data ingestion goes back into model optimization. As a result, it is creating a data-based moat that improves the TPUs' efficiency on a forward basis. The last factor is external monetization (Anthropic is using 1 million TPUs). Google is now monetizing its silicon advantage by renting it to competitors and rebuilding a cost center (infrastructure) into a high-margin revenue that hedges against Nvidia’s market grip.

As per Bank of America (BAC) report, Gemini’s daily active user growth (+17% MoM) is larger than ChatGPT’s (+2% MoM). It is indicating that the growth momentum for OpenAI is flattening, and Google’s AI integration is getting higher-paced. The addition of 9 million daily active users for Gemini versus only 6 million for ChatGPT is an clear indication that Google’s Interface Layer is holding on to its 2.7 billion daily search visits and leading to a 351% YoY hyper-surge in Gemini usage. This confirms that Google’s AI products are functioning as an additive engagement layer. It is now a super-app extension that is creating a dual-moat structure as Google retains the high-margin query volume and captures the high-complexity compute volume.

Furthermore, this trend supports my Inference Cost Arbitrage argument. By shifting 62 million daily active users onto internal 7th Generation TPUs, Google is actively driving its marginal cost of inference toward zero, while competitors like OpenAI (whose traffic is stalling) and Bing (whose traffic is declining 9%). The divergence in traffic (the 24% MoM rise in Gemini visits against the contraction in the other platforms) suggests that Google’s aggressive deployment of AI Overviews and progressive training of user behavior to prefer multimodal (integrated answers over standalone chatbots) thereby re-closing the loop on the digital advertising monopoly before competitors can reach a critical mass.

Moreover, this hardware advantage extends to energy. The Project Suncatcher initiative and nuclear deals support Google to decouple from the commercial energy grid. By vertically integrating energy > silicon > model > application, Google constructs a forward margin profile that Wall Street models (that simply extrapolate current CapEx trends) are failing to project. When Wall Street completely realizes that Google’s AI CapEx is actually purchasing deflationary assets (chips/TPUs that lower forward OpEx) rather than inflationary assets (chips/GPUs that depreciate rapidly), GOOG/GOOGL stock will re-rate from an ad/media utility multiple to a hard-tech infrastructure multiple, in my opinion.

If I apply AI-adjusted PE multiple and EPS as the TPU margin advantage materializes, GOOG stock can be valued towards the high-end of P/E multiples (forward and TTM) expansion to 29x-32x by 2026’s end. The use of the high-end consensus FY2026 EPS of $13.11 (considering the EPS revision trend) with 13% mid-term average surprise-% (quarterly, since the Q1-2023 AI phase) provides a 2026 GOOG target price range of $429-$474 in line with the $427 Fibonacci target. However, on an FY2027 basis, with EPS growing to ~$14.13 (high end, based on buybacks and margin expansion), that range can be $463-$511 interim to $599 Fibonacci target. I am not using the DCF model here, as it heavily penalizes Alphabet stock for its $90B+ CapEx spend (treating it as a drag on FCF). With improving ROIC, ROA, and ROE, the valuation penalty/cap will not be there.

The Agentic Disintermediation of Ads

For GOOG/GOOGL stock price, antitrust regulation is not a major forward negative (as it takes years and often results in impotent fines). It is the structural collapse of the Ten Blue Links business model due to Agentic AI. The logic behind disintermediation is that Google's trillion-dollar business is built on a specific user behavior [query to list of links to user selection to click (ad revenue)], however, the shift to Agentic AI (through Project Jarvis and Gemini) breaks this chain. I am trying to say that if a user asks Gemini (or OpenAI, or Siri-Apple Intelligence) to ‘Book a flight to London under $600’ and the AI agent can execute directly via an API. Hence, the user will not see a search results page and so they will also not see a sponsored link/display ad. As a result, the impression volume (main metric of the Google Network and YouTube Ads) can theoretically collapse even if query volume increases.

Why is this an important risk? Because AI Overviews increase engagement, but they gloss over the monetization density of these overviews. As of now, Google monetizes intent. However, Agentic AI fulfills intent without the intermediate step of shopping for a solution. If Google shifts from a high-margin auction model (selling clicks) to a lower-margin transaction model (taking a % of the booking), profitability per query can drop. Along with that, Alphabet faces the innovator's dilemma. To win in AI, it must deploy agents that actively reduce the surface area for core ad products. If it doesn't, OpenAI and others will. To support this, what I see as problematic is the decline in Google Network revenue (-2.6% YoY, in Q3). It indicates that third-party web properties (where Google serves ads) are already losing traffic to zero-click AI answers. Logically, if this trend accelerates and bleeds into core Search, the valuation-multiple compression can be severe. If quarterly earnings in 2026 show that AI-Mode queries are monetizing at lower rates than traditional search queries, GOOG stock can retest the $234 and $173 levels.

Alphabet Pre-Q4 And Post-Q4 Earnings Alpha Protocol

Pre-Q4 Earnings [The Ironwood Accumulation Phase (Current to 3 February 2026)]

In the immediate weeks leading up to the Q4 earnings, bulls can aggressively position for a volatility compression squeeze above $295. The market is in a price discovery mode for the efficacy of Google’s TPU Ironwood. The move here is to front-run the possible analyst revisions that will occur once the Ironwood efficiency gains are registered on the Q4 call. Alphabet expects 2025 CapEx to land between $91 billion-$93 billion. The market can interpret this as a cost burden. However, my thesis marks this as a deflationary asset acquisition. Hence, any sudden price weakness based on AI Bubble-FOMO means to buy more shares as near to $295 support as possible. The market is not focused on the margin accretion/leap that results from shifting workloads from Nvidia H100s (high OpEx/CapEx) to internal Ironwood TPUs (lower OpEx/CapEx). The period prior to Q4 is about betting on the industrialization of inference.

The Earnings Event: The Capex Shock Paradox

When Q4 results are announced, the numbers (revenue/EPS) can be secondary to the 2026 CapEx outlook. Alphabet already signaled (in the Q3-2025 call) a big increase in 2026 CapEx. An algorithmic sell-off is highly probable if the 2026 CapEx outlook exceeds decisively without a side-by-side jump in Cloud revenue. This volatility event represents the entry point within the Trend Pivot's Range of Influence. The major metric to watch during Q4 earnings is the Cloud Operating Margin. In Q3, Cloud operating margin expanded to ~24% (up from 17% of Q3-2024). If Alphabet guides for huge 2026 CapEx but projects stable/expanding Cloud margins, it proves (mathematically) that internal silicon (TPUs) is decoupling the cost structure from the rest of the industry. Therefore, interpret a Capex Shock with margin stability as the main buy signal.

Post-Q4 Earnings [The Waymo And Agentic Realization (February 2026 - Mid 2026)]

Following the Q4 call, the market’s view may shift from infrastructure costs to asset realization. The post-earnings bulls must focus on valuing the hidden assets that are approaching green escape, like Waymo’s expansion into Tokyo, London, Miami, Dallas, Houston, and San Antonio, alongside 1 million rides/month. Post-Q4, bulls must monitor for a spinoff signal/a separate disclosure for Waymo that can instantly realize more than $100 billion in enterprise value trapped in the Other Bets valuation drag. Moreover, as Agentic AI (Gemini acting on behalf of users) moves from testing to deployment, GOOG/GOOGL stock will re-rate based on Service Revenue rather than Ad Revenue. If post-earnings data ticks that AI-Mode queries are monetizing through transaction fees (like booking a flight directly) at a rate comparable to traditional Search ads, the risk of disintermediation can become inactive.

_

Overall, my stance on GOOG/GOOGL is Aggressive Long and Accumulate More on Dips. The merging of the technical breakout structure with the inflection of the Ironwood TPU cycle creates a big opportunity with forward monitorables: a stable breakout above the swing high of $330 (to confirm the impulse phase toward $427), Q4 operating margin in Google Cloud [if this crosses 25% (currently ~24%), it can confirm that the TPU cost advantage is real], and Other Bets losses (any reduction here through Waymo commercialization is pure profit upside).

data.title: Love Direct Deposit? You'll Love Automatic Data Processing
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

How often do you get paid?

For most, they get paid every two weeks. However, some of us get paid on a monthly basis or even a weekly basis. Some companies offer you the option to cash out at the end of every shift if you want! We've grown to love and expect that deposit landing in our bank account exactly when the company promises it will.

It wasn't that long ago that paychecks were physical checks that you would have to run down to your local bank branch to deposit. I can remember that my dad picked his bank account because the branch was just down the street from where he worked. He would get his paycheck every two weeks, drive to the bank, and deposit it on his way home. Now, though, the vast majority of us receive that deposit electronically. Those electronic deposits are processed primarily through some form of payroll software.

Part of my mission in building a dividend-paying portfolio is getting paid by everyday activities that Americans, Canadians, and Europeans enjoy regularly. One thing that I believe we all enjoy is getting paid.

So the question then becomes, how do you get paid for other people getting paid? Today, we're going to answer that question.

Let's dive in!

This Dividend King is on Sale Now

Automatic Data Processing (ADP) is the world's largest human capital management software company. Across its 1.1 million clients and counting, ADP is responsible for paying more than 42 million employees around the globe.

What is the appeal of ADP? The company's software and services are essential to operate in the business world today. Any company with employees must do payroll, keep track of time and attendance, and administer benefits. Few companies have the time or desire to do these things in-house, so many let the likes of ADP handle them.

Looking ahead, the company believes that it can generate 6% to 7% annual revenue growth over the medium term. ADP's path to doing so includes expanding its global footprint and adapting to changing client needs to maintain its business bookings growth momentum. What's more, the company is focusing on enhancing its ADP Assist platform to drive client engagement. In the last year, ADP Assist has helped with over 5.5 million client conversations, according to President and CEO Maria Black's opening remarks during the Q1 2026 Earnings Call.

Strategic investments aimed at improving operating efficiency should further bolster ADP's profit margins. Along with modest share repurchases (1% annual share count reductions), this is how the company expects to generate 9% to 11% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth in the coming years.

That jives with the FAST Graphs analyst consensus of 9.3% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth through FY 2028, off a FY 2025 base of $10.01.

Financially, ADP is a fortress. The company maintains an AA- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

ADP's 2.7% dividend yield is reasonably attractive. This is a great yield on cost play in my opinion. That's because ADP has grown its dividend by 11.5% annually over the last five years. This equates to a Chowder Number of around 14%. The payout ratio is set to be in the high-50% range, which should allow ADP to lengthen its half-century dividend growth streak.

In my view, ADP is a classic example of a great business trading at an enticing valuation. At the current $258 share price, it's priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 22.6 (52% weight for FY 2026 and a 48% weight for FY 2027).

For more color, ADP's 10-year average P/E ratio is 28.6. I think that this remains a sensible fair value multiple. That's because its 10% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth target (the midpoint of 9-11%) isn't far off its 10-year average CAGR of 13.2%. As Kody noted in his previous article on ADP, the company is more profitable now than it has been in years past. This also backs up my fair value per share estimate of $326, which points to a 21% discount to fair value.

If ADP lives up to expectations and reverts to fair value, that would result in a 31% upside through calendar year 2026. By the end of FY 2031, ADP could log 15% annual total returns.

The HCM software company still faces risks, though. As Kody indicated in his September article, noteworthy risks to ADP include the potential for a major cyber breach and a competitive industry landscape.

Any event that tarnishes its brand image among clients could materially hurt its fundamentals. That's because, in such a case, ADP could be hit by an exodus of clients and regulatory penalties/litigation.

The company also operates in a highly fragmented industry. If it can't keep up with ever-changing client preferences, it could lose market share to competitors. This could also diminish its growth story.

Conclusion

ADP is a fantastic business that engages in an activity that we all enjoy tremendously. If you look forward to payday, you look forward to ADP potentially providing services to the company you work for, which makes that essential function operate. Very few people pay attention to their direct deposit, outside its amount, until it's missing. If it goes missing, all eyes turn to the company you work for, and their eyes turn to the software they use.

When everything runs smoothly, no one minds paying for the payroll software to achieve the results that they're looking for. Happy employees are productive employees. Productive employees drive strong earnings for the company they work for. Using ADP allows companies to keep employees happy by ensuring their payroll deposits arrive on time. ADP makes investors happy by providing them with strong earnings growth and strong dividend growth.

This is yet another wonderful way you can invest like a Dividend King and enjoy income from every single aspect of the economy and daily life. No need to chase the latest craze or the biggest buzzwords in the market. Forget the confusion and find clarity.

data.title: Alpha Pro Tech: Reliable Assets, Minimal Liabilities
data.description:
Introduction

Alpha Pro Tech (APT) is an obscure company. However, as any seasoned value investor would know, seemingly unexciting companies often, paradoxically, present the investor with the most exciting opportunities. APT has the potential to be one of these opportunities. Indeed, when conducting a hyper-conservative liquidation valuation, it could be argued that the company is, or at least has the potential to be, cheap. But when digging into the balance sheet a little further, the investor might come to realize that the business is even cheaper than a valuation based on the book value of its assets might suggest. When also considered in the context of the favorable qualitative aspects of the business, the investor may be willing to forgo some cheapness and still acquire shares at a reasonable price.

Business Summary

Alpha Pro Tech was founded in 1983 and is headquartered in Ontario, Canada. Alpha Pro Tech Ltd. is the parent company of subsidiaries Alpha Pro Tech, Inc., which manufactures and sells "disposable protective apparel and infection control products for the cleanroom, industrial, pharmaceutical, medical, and dental markets", and Alpha Pro Tech Engineered Products, which manufactures building supply construction weatherization products. Resultingly, the company has two segments: Disposable Protective Apparel (DPA) and Building Supply. The below image shows the company's products under their respective segments.

In full year 2024, Building Supply constituted 62% of revenue, with Disposable Protective Apparel making up the rest. Almost all of the company's revenues are generated in the U.S., with less than 1% coming from international customers in 2024. Information on the company's manufacturing base can be seen below.

Manufacturing Location Product
Valdosta, Georgia (Leased) Building Supply products
India (joint venture, 41.66% ownership) Building Supply & Disposable Protective Apparel products
Nogales, Arizona (Leased) Disposable Protective Apparel
Asia, Mexico (3rd party contract manufacturer) Disposable Protective Apparel

Valuation and Thesis

APT has a very healthy balance sheet. The company has a book value of $6.08 according to Seeking Alpha, which consists entirely of tangible assets. APT's net current asset value (NCAV) is roughly $3.90 as of the latest 10-Q filing. Importantly, the company's current assets are made up of 34% cash/equivalents, 45% inventory, 14% receivables, and 7% prepaid expenses. Since the majority of APT's current assets are in inventory and receivables, we cannot blindly take NCAV at book value, as the book value of inventory and receivables may be more than its actual liquidation value, which is what the NCAV calculation represents.

Of course, we could take the old Benjamin Graham approach of net-net working capital (NNWC) to value this, and discount inventory and receivables at uniform rates of 75% and 50%, respectively. With these adjustments, APT's NNWC value sits at $2.50, which, realistically, might be too conservative in light of the company's long history of stable operations and consistent profitability. The company has also not traded this low since 2017, making a return to that price anytime soon unlikely, especially when considering the share buybacks and resultant share count reduction since then.

Also, APT's inventory likely has a low risk of obsolescence. APT's inventory is broken up between the Building Supply and Disposable Protective Apparel segments. As of the most recent quarterly results, APT reports that the Building Supply segment's inventory book value is $11.9 million, whereas the Disposable Protective Apparel's book value is about $11.6 million. This is a roughly 50/50 split.

As can be seen above, the Building Supply segment consists primarily of inventory related to house wraps and synthetic roof underlayment. Intuitively, it would make sense that the sales generated from the Building Supply segment would correlate with housing starts in the U.S. Let's see how this has panned out over the past five years.

Year U.S. Housing Starts Growth Building Supply Sales Growth
2020 7% 15.1%
2021 16% 20.6%
2022 -3% 0.1%
2023 -8.5% 9.4%
2024 -3.7% -11%
2025 (3Q YoY) -2.6% 5.4%

In most years, save 2024, APT's Building Supply sales growth seems to have outperformed that of the housing starts in the U.S. One reason for this may be the growing demand for renovation and maintenance that is required as existing houses age, which increases secular demand for APT's Building Supply products.

Furthermore, the products that make up APT's Building Supply inventory are largely non-perishable and not susceptible to erratically changing consumer tastes. This is further corroborated by the lack of substantial inventory write-downs that have occurred over the past decade or so, as well as stable gross margins over the same period. Indeed, even during the 2008 housing crash, there were no inventory write downs. Keeping this in mind, let's apply a conservative 80% discount to APT's Building Supply inventory.

The other 50% of APT's inventory belongs to the Disposable Protective Apparel segment. A large portion of this inventory consists of face masks, which saw a considerable rise in demand during the COVID-19 pandemic. This rise in demand correlated with a large build-up and subsequent tapering of inventory, as can be seen in the below table, starting at the peak year of 2021.

Year DPA Inventory (millions)
2021 $16.24
2022 $14.39
2023 $13.16
2024 $11.8
2025 (3Q) $11.58

This means that APT has been successful in selling off their DPA inventory since the COVID-19 peak without having to resort to inventory write downs which would indicate obsolescence. Profitability has remained stable during this time as well, indicating that APT did not have to significantly discount its inventory to sell it. This makes sense, as the DPA portion of APT's inventory, like the Building Supply segment, is made up of non-perishable items such as face masks and coveralls, which are insulated from both fickle consumer tastes and physical spoilage. An 80% discount to this inventory segment seems reasonable as well.

APT does not seem to have had any previous issues in turning their accounts receivable into cash. Indeed, according to their full year 2024 10-k, their allowance for doubtful accounts sat at $35,000 against an accounts receivable figure of $4.89 million, a rate of less than 1%. Regardless, to remain conservative, we will discount this account by 10%. We will also disregard the prepaid expense account entirely. Let's revisit our valuation above after applying these discounts.

Account Value (millions)
Cash/Equivalents $17.7(1) = $17.7
Inventory $23.5(0.8) = $18.8
Receivables $7.1(0.9) = $6.39
Prepaid Expenses $3.5(0) = $0
Sum $42.89 million

Taking our current asset value of $42.89 million and subtracting total liabilities of $11.3 million gives us $31.59 million. Dividing this number by shares outstanding of 10.4 million gives us an NCAV per share of roughly $3.05. Again, APT has not traded this cheaply since 2017.

Of course, this valuation has to be taken into the context of both the realities of the business and the alternatives the prospective investor has available to him. The NCAV method is an inherently conservative assessment of liquidation value, as it pays no heed to long-term assets such as property, plant, and equipment, or long-term investments. But the chances of APT, a healthy and profitable enterprise, liquidating its assets anytime soon are decidedly low. Furthermore, the company's management has been aggressively buying back shares, something that may cause an investor like Mohnish Pabrai to dub it a "cannibal".

These positive traits could act as an additional margin of safety for the company. In other words, the investor could probably justify purchasing shares of APT at a premium to its liquidation value, given the financial health of the firm and the evident willingness of management to return capital to shareholders.

It also needs to be noted that APT does not have any debt on its balance sheet. In fact, $8.2 million of the $11.3 million liabilities on APT's balance sheet are lease liabilities. Practically speaking, in the (unlikely) event of an actual liquidation, APT would probably not be required to pay back the entirety of its long-term lease obligations, as the landlord's claim to the remaining lease payments would likely be capped. It therefore might be reasonable to significantly discount this liability account on the balance sheet, which would meaningfully increase APT's NCAV value.

Let's unpack this further. Assuming APT decides to liquidate its assets, and further assuming, for argument's sake, that the amount of the lease liability the landlord can claim is consistent with bankruptcy code 502(b)(6), the landlord would be looking at a claim of 15% of the lease liability. In APT's case, this 15% would equate to $1.23 million, and would reduce total liabilities to about $4.3 million. Redoing the NCAV calculation, but without discounting any current assets, and discounting the lease liabilities account by 85%, gives us a much less conservative (and likely more realistic) NCAV of roughly $4.50.

But alas, the purpose of the NCAV valuation method is not to be precise, but to provide a sizable margin of safety to protect the investor against his own ignorance and miscalculations. As Buffett says, "It's better to be approximately right than precisely wrong". With this in mind, let's approximate the midpoint between our conservative and not-as-conservative valuations above and settle at a target price of $3.75. One could probably make a convincing argument to purchase shares above this price as well, but $3.75 and below would put this in the 'Strong Buy' category. For now, it's a tentative 'buy' at and below the current price of $4.50.

These ratings might change, however, based on the opportunity cost associated with favoring APT over alternative investments. If there are no other attractive investments available, buying APT at a loftier price might be a good allocation of capital, but if there are cheaper alternatives, waiting for APT to reach a cheaper price might be the preferred approach.

Risks

As the bulk of our valuation is based on APT's inventory, the primary risk to our investment thesis is this inventory being worth less than what we valued it at. This might happen for several reasons. If, for example, a building code changes that causes a significant portion of APT's roof underlays to become obsolete, or if the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NOSHA) abruptly changes a safety standard for APT's N-95 face masks, inventory write-downs would have to be recorded, diminishing book value and ultimately eroding the investor's margin of safety. This risk seems to be unlikely, however, as there has been no evidence of significant inventory impairments in the country's extensive history.

The investor should also keep a vigilant eye out for any telltale signs that might indicate a more subtle decline in inventory value, such as shrinking gross margins without a corresponding increase in the cost of goods sold. This might signal that inventory is being sold at a discount due to softening demand or price competition, which would also be evident in diminishing cash flows. Fortunately for the investor, APT has had reasonably stable gross margins over the past ten years, making any potential anomalies easy to detect and investigate in a proactive manner. Even in a worst-case scenario, if the investor manages to purchase shares at a low enough price, the margin of safety should be sufficient to protect against any downside that may otherwise occur.

Conclusion

APT is a financially sound company with reliable assets and potentially overstated liabilities. While it's important to remain as conservative as possible as a value investor, it is perhaps equally important to be reasonable. If we were to blindly apply Ben Graham's NNWC formula to account for APT's inventory-dominant current asset position, we would come up with a conservative, but probably unreasonably conservative, valuation considering the favorable qualities of the business. With a slightly more aggressive approach to valuation, the investor can land on a price target that is more realistic while remaining, on the whole, reasonably conservative. Buying at or below the identified price target should insulate the investor from any overwhelming downside if a worst-case scenario were to occur, while at the same time keeping open the possibility of capturing any upside that becomes manifest from the market recognizing the company's true value.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How A $600,000 Portfolio Can Produce $5,000 In Monthly Dividends
data.description:
Index-Linked Monthly Income

I can confidently claim that this is the easiest time in modern history to be an income investor. Traditional investing advice usually follows the path of the 4% rule, which requires investors to actively sell off 4% of their portfolio every year to support lifestyle expenses. What if you didn't want to deal with the hassle of selling shares, especially during a downturn? What if you aren't able to accumulate millions of dollars in order to have a reasonably funded retirement?

What if there was a way to collect a monthly stream of dividend income, while still maintaining direct equity exposure to constituents of both the S&P 500 Index (SPX) and the Nasdaq-100 (NDX)? The good news is that this is now a possibility for investors, and it is one of the most straightforward ways to fund a stress-free retirement. I believe that a combination of these funds is attractive because they offer:

  1. Diversification away from single-stock holdings.
  2. Potential for capital appreciation.
  3. Tax-efficient dividend income.
  4. Resilience against shifts in interest rates.
  5. A monthly stream of dividend income.
  6. High but sustainable dividend yields.

A decade ago there were a lot fewer options for investors that wanted to create a stream of dividend income. This required investors to increase exposure to real estate securities, which can show sensitivity to interest rate shifts. Perhaps investors could allocate capital towards business development companies, which depended on a healthy debt environment. Or maybe an allocation to junk bonds, which can potentially expose investors to funds that have higher fees and exposure to default risks.

Over the last few years, there has been a rise in covered call ETFs. These are funds that write options against a portion of their assets in order to generate amplified income levels. The option premiums are earned and then paid out to investors as a distribution. The unique part about these funds is that they can still provide exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world, which makes it possible to get direct equity exposure that is similar to more traditional index-tracking ETFs.

Who Is This Portfolio For?

Accumulating $600,000 in invested capital is no easy task because it requires a larger time commitment for many of us. I believe there are two primary methods for investing. The first method is the most popular approach of investing in large-cap index funds to capture growth and ride the positive momentum of the market. By maintaining a position in an ETF that tracks the S&P 500, such as the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), you can get instant diversification across the highest quality businesses in the world. If you are a younger investor, positioning yourself for growth makes the most sense since income investments tend to underperform indices.

The second method is to invest for income, which can be achieved through different avenues, but is usually a great fit for investors that prefer to see their invested capital produce cash flow over time. The challenge with this second approach is that it can be challenging to know which asset class is the best fit for you. You can choose the classic dividend growth route, but this requires you to actively manage multiple positions. You can choose to go the route of higher yielding alternative asset classes, such as REITs or business development companies, but these introduce sector specific risks and may not be the most tax-efficient approach.

I believe that the highest quality covered call ETFs can solve these challenges, and therefore this portfolio allocation is for investors that prefer the second approach. This approach is usually aligned for investors that are nearing or at retirement, since additional income generation during a period of your life where you may no longer be actively working is something that is extremely beneficial. The income collected from your portfolio is a great supplement to other traditional sources like social security or pensions. The standout thing about this portfolio allocation, is that it can provide a hybrid approach between the two investing paths.

In an earlier published article titled, 'The Blueprint To Navigate Option ETFs Through 2026', I laid out the positive traits that make an option ETF a great long-term holding. In summary, I believe that the following characteristics lead to the most ideal scenario for investors that want an efficient way to generate income within their portfolio.

  • Provide direct equity exposure to constituents of an index.
  • Dynamic option strategy that changes based on market conditions.
  • Options that aren't written against the entire value of a portfolio.
  • Low expense ratio relative to peers.
  • Can provide a hedge during market declines.

Portfolio Allocation

Investment %Weight $Amount %Yield Estimated Annual Income
Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) 25% $150,000 ~8% $12,000
Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) 25% $150,000 ~9.8% $14,700
Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) 25% $150,000 ~11.6% $17,400
NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) 25% $150,000 ~13.7% $20,550
Totals 100% $600,000 ~10.7% $64,650

As we can see, the $600,000 is allocated across only four positions. With an equal allocation, we are able to achieve an estimated annual income of ~$64,650. This translates to an expected monthly income of $5,387 for investors, which is above the U.S. average monthly income of about $5,200.

The idea here is to link your income to the safety of the indexes. What's great about all of the listed ETFs is that they all track an underlying index and directly own the common shares of the equities within the index. Since we are getting exposure to the indices, we can see that the portfolio allocation still provides diversification across sectors. While there is an emphasis on technology, this is just the result of the fact that indices currently lean heavily on technology as well.

Additionally, these ETFs implement dynamic option strategies that allow them to directly tweak their strategy based on market conditions. All of these ETFs implement an OTM (out-of-the-money) option approach, which means that the strike price is typically above the current market price of the index. This OTM strategy allows for a greater level of capital appreciation to occur, compared to option ETFs that implement an ATM (at-the-money) option strategy. ATM option ETFs set a lower strike price, which limits growth potential but can lead to higher option premiums. However, the Goldman ETFs have a different methodology to their option writing versus the NEOS funds. Each of the fund's strategies can be summarized as follows:

Ticker Option Summary Options Days To Expiry
GPIX Writes options on the S&P 500 and dynamically adjusts the percentage of the portfolio it writes options against, between (25% -75%). Low volatility = larger portion of options written. High volatility = lower portion of options written ~30 Days
GPIQ Writes options on the Nasdaq-100 and dynamically adjusts the percentage of the portfolio it writes options against, between (25% -75%). Low volatility = larger portion of options written. High volatility = lower portion of options written ~30 Days
SPYI Writes options on the S&P 500 and dynamically adjusts the strike price of the options based on market momentum. Higher trending markets = higher strike prices ~30 Days
QQQI Writes options on the Nasdaq-100 and dynamically adjusts the strike price of the options based on market momentum. ~30 Days

As we can see, these funds have flexibility with their option writing strategies. In short, they will reduce the reliance on option writing during strong bull markets in order to capture more upside. If the market is growing slower, these funds will not capture much of the upside growth, but the option writing may become more crucial to its ability to provide favorable returns and consistent distributions.

These ETFs all issue payouts on a monthly basis, so investors can expect frequent distributions. However, the primary bonus here is that the ETFs tend to prioritize tax-efficiency over time through the use of return of capital. This form of dividend income is a bit unique because return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. If you are a retired investor and plan on living off the supplemental income generated within your portfolio, you can effectively maintain a position in these ETFs with the intent to never sell.

However, tax-efficient distributions don't necessarily equate to tax-free. There may still be portions of the distributions that are funded by net investment income. I've compiled the latest section19(a) notices from each of these ETFs, which is the easiest way to know how much of the distribution is made up of return of capital:

Goldman Sachs ETFs

  • GPIX: 89.9% return of capital.
  • GPIQ: 96.4% return of capital.

NEOS ETFs

  • SPYI: 95% return of capital.
  • QQQI: 100% return of capital.

To help conceptualize how return of capital works, let's imagine that you buy a stock at $10 per share and it issues a $1 distribution using return of capital. Once that distribution is paid out, the investor's cost basis would be reduced down to $9 per share. So in theory it is technically possible to maintain a position for long enough until your cost basis gets dropped down to $0 per share. At this point, all distributions received going forward would be classified as long-term capital appreciation, which still has favorable tax treatment for most investors.

However, the downside of return of capital distributions is that every distribution directly reduces the NAV (net asset value) of the funds over time. So if markets are trending flat or slightly down, the large payouts from these ETFs may cause the underlying NAV of these funds to decline. During a period of unfavorable market conditions, this concept can lead to an investor's capital eroding over time. This is just the nature of these higher-yield assets; since the distributions are so large, there may be scenarios where the ETFs are distributing more than they actually earn in option premiums. This may be done in order to keep payouts consistent and decrease the variability of payouts. Depending on how severe of a market downturn that takes places, it's very likely that these ETFs will decrease payouts to limit the level of NAV erosion that is experienced.

Risks

When it comes to the risk profile, the problem is that these funds are very likely to underperform traditional index-tracking ETFs over a longer holding period. Although the respective option strategies allow the ETFs to capture the positive momentum of the market, they still cap the upside gains to whatever the selected strike prices are. This is why this portfolio allocation is best suited for retired investors that want to put a specific emphasis on income generation, rather than market growth. Overall, it makes more sense for retirees to prioritize income stability over anything else, so this underperformance risk is more of an issue for younger investors.

Unfortunately, these funds are still relatively new, so we have a very limited operating window to compare their performances. The limited operating window can also be a risk in itself since we don't have many time frames to measure how these funds will perform in a downturn. However, we can use the brief market decline in early 2025 as an example. Markets sharply dropped in response to President Trump's initial tariff proposals. As we can see below, each of the four ETFs shared a similar decline to the market's bottom between the start of 2025 and April 8th, 2025. The challenge is that these ETFs will not be able to fully recover from sharp declines like this due to the capped nature of the option-writing strategy.

Additionally, some investors may not like the fact that these ETFs are closely linked to the indices. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100 are overwhelmingly allocated to technology companies. While the technology sector continues to expand due to the growing AI demand, sentiment of the sector can change rapidly and cause a shift in momentum. If the technology sector experiences a downturn, these ETFs may not necessarily provide any resilience against other high-yield assets that may take another approach.

For instance, other high-yield assets, such as REITs or business development companies, may be appealing because their resilience and income potential is disconnected from the overall trend of the market. Since these option ETFs are primarily allocated to large-cap US assets, there may be less diversity than some investors prefer.

What we do know is that the actual payout amounts may vary in the scenario where markets are in a prolonged downtrend. The distribution amounts are determined by the success of the written options, implied volatility, and the overall direction of the market indices.

Therefore, this approach may not be ideal for investors that prefer a consistent and easy-to-predict stream of income over time. If markets were to maintain a prolonged downtrend, the payouts received from these ETFs may also decline. If the funds simply produce a lower level of option premiums, the distributions will be adjusted downward to offset.

A good example of this was briefly demonstrated in early 2025 when markets dropped in reaction to President Trump's tariff proposals. Using GPIQ as an example below, we can see how payouts were slightly impacted, before eventually rising back to their prior levels once markets recovered. However, all of these ETFs have been able to keep payouts in a very consistent range over their lifespan so far.

Lastly, these ETFs are very unlikely to produce any sort of dividend growth that would be similar to a traditional ETF. Therefore, this may require investors to reinvest a portion of their income back into these assets in order to offset any periods where payouts are reduced. So this weakness may require some active involvement and attention through periods of elevated market uncertainty.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that a $600,000 portfolio allocation across these high-quality option ETFs is enough to provide a minimum of $5,000 in monthly income, which is enough to rival the average monthly income in the U.S. These ETFs are great because they keep your portfolio aligned to the market indices and allow investors to ride the momentum of the market over time. These ETFs provide direct equity exposure to the constituents of the indices, which means that shareholders are getting exposure to the highest quality businesses in the world. Additionally, each of their option strategies allows these funds to experience capital appreciation over time. Most importantly, these ETFs tend to prioritize tax-efficient distributions, which is a huge relief for investors using their allocation as a supplemental form of retirement income.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street closed out the first full trading week of the new year at record levels on Friday, with the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) ending about 34 points shy of the historic 7,000 mark. A revival in the tech trade, coupled with mixed labor market economic data, boosted sentiment and offset geopolitical escalation between the U.S. and Venezuela.

Oil prices (CL1:COM)(CO1:COM) logged gains for the week, despite the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro by U.S. forces. President Donald Trump exhorted oil companies to make public pledges of investments in the South American nation's oil industry, but received few commitments.

Turning to the tech trade, investors welcomed announcements from major firms such as Nvidia (NVDA), CoreWeave (CRWV), and AMD (AMD) at the CES event in Las Vegas.

Looking at this week's economic data, several labor market updates presented a mixed picture, denting expectations for a Federal Reserve interest rate cut later this month. U.S. private sector employment bounced back in December 2025, but still missed consensus estimates. Job openings fell in November 2025, also missing expectations. On the other hand, job cuts in December 2025 dropped to their lowest level since July 2024. The data culminated in the December jobs report, which was softer than anticipated.
